hypothesis: Replay observations are faithful enough to run the agent directly, and outcome/strength-weighted agreement with real players gives a discriminating, Kaggle-correlated evaluation axis.
reason: The v0-05d6 rule agent was run on 488,455 real replay decision states (142,599 with an alakazam actor) with zero agent exceptions and zero bad-shape outputs, so faithful agent-vs-replay agreement is feasible. However, aggregate agreement (~0.251) is essentially flat and non-monotonic across player strength and game outcome, so outcome/strength weighting does NOT yield a usable strength gradient. The real, actionable signal is at the context level, where three contexts are systematically BELOW random alignment.

outcome_strength_NOT_discriminating.note: top10 players agree LEAST with our agent; the rank spread exists but is non-monotonic, so aggregate agreement is not a clean strength signal. Conditioning on winners/top200 adds essentially nothing. The user-chosen outcome-weighting hypothesis is empirically refuted as a discriminator.

split_stability.note: stable across deterministic splits; holdout consistent.

below_random_systematic_anti_alignment.interpretation: Bench placement: our agent picks differently from humans FAR below chance. High-volume, high-confidence weakness.
Card-return-to-deck selection: near-zero alignment, well below chance.
Setup-phase bench placement: below chance; small volume.

high_volume_low_lift.interpretation: Main-phase action: above random but low; dominant volume; long-term high-impact but high-branching and hard.

key_finding: This diagnostic gives a context-level improvement roadmap, not an aggregate strength gate. The single most actionable, high-confidence target is TO_BENCH (and the related TO_DECK / SETUP_BENCH): our agent's bench/return-to-deck choices are systematically below random alignment with real players, which is almost certainly a real weakness rather than a stylistic difference.
implications_for_next_version: Do NOT pursue outcome-weighted imitation as a strength lever; the winner/loser and rank gradients are flat/non-monotonic.

v0-06d7 candidate: fix the TO_BENCH bench-placement logic in the v0-05d6 rule agent, validating against this context-level agreement metric (agreement should move toward / above random and toward winner choices) plus the existing safety/holdout/eval gates. Single hypothesis, one context.
Keep MAIN as a later, larger target once a measurement loop on the low-volume contexts is proven.
next_candidates: v0-06d7: TO_BENCH bench-placement rule fix on v0-05d6 base, gated on replay-agreement (context-level) + safety.
Later: MAIN-phase action scoring, using a per-option agreement decomposition.
